figures.caption: Fig. 1: Approximation of the Styblinski–Tang function fST (x, y) = 1 2 (x 4−16x2+5x+ y4− 16y2+5y) using zeroth-, first-, and second-order augmentation, at the minimum training set size achieving a prescribed accuracy on a validation set.

Fig. 2: Relative L2, H1, and H2 errors for the approximation of the 12-dimensional test (4.1) as a function of N , using IHC(4) with q = 3,482. Zeroth-, first-, and secondorder regressions are compared, with partial Hessian fractions ρ ∈ {0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0}. Curves are averaged over 10 independent training sets. Note that each panel reports a different error metric, identified by the subscript of Err, and that the ordinate scales differ accordingly across panels.
Fig. 3: Polynomial approximation of the value function for the 2D Van der Pol problem with N = 25 training samples (red dots) under zeroth-, first-, and second-order augmentation.

Fig. 4: Relative validation errors as functions of N for the enriched basis IHC(4) ∪ ITD(10) (q = 78), averaged over 100 Monte Carlo realisations. Weights γ1 = γ2 = 1. Note that each panel reports a different error metric, identified by the subscript of Err, and that the ordinate scales differ accordingly across panels.

Fig. 5: Closed-loop trajectories for N = 15 training samples. Each row corresponds to an initial condition x0 ∈ {(2,−1), (2, 1)}. Left: phase portrait. Centre: state norm ∥y(t)∥2. Right: control signal u(t), clipped to [−25, 25].

Fig. 6: Relative approximation errors as a function of basis cardinality q, for four values of N and three error metrics. The total number of equations M is indicated in each legend. Partial second-order formulations with ρ ∈ {0.3, 0.5} are included. Each point is the mean over 100 Monte Carlo realisations; λ = 10−10, γ1 = γ2 = 1. Note that each column reports a different error metric, identified by the subscript of Err, and that the ordinate scales differ accordingly across panels.

Table 2: Best performance attainable by each derivative order at the minimum N required by second-order augmentation to satisfy (4.7). Basis selected independently per order to minimise p95 of the mean control error. Asterisk: q > N . The ratios Mk/q give equations per unknown at each derivative order; M/q is the overall overdetermination ratio.

Fig. 7: Relative L2, H1, H2 errors and HJB residual for the 6D satellite problem as a function of N , for bases IHC(4) ∪ ITD(5) (q = 663, top row) and IHC(4) ∪ ITD(7) (q = 1,800, bottom row). Hessian fractions ρ ∈ {0.3, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0} included for secondorder regression. Curves averaged over 50 Monte Carlo realisations. Note that each panel reports a different error metric, identified by the subscript of Err (or the HJB residual), and that the ordinate scales differ accordingly across panels.

Table 3: Best basis of the form IHC(4) ∪ ITD(s) for each N , selected to minimise ErrL2 over s ∈ {5, . . . , 11}. The ratio M/q = 28N/q measures the degree of overdetermination. All errors averaged over 30 Monte Carlo realisations.

Fig. 8: Closed-loop trajectories for the 6D satellite using partial Hessian regression (ρ = 0.5, N = 70, IHC(4), q = 537), from IC1 = (0.5, 0.2,−0.3, 0.1,−0.1, 0.1) (top) and IC2 = (−0.6, 0.2,−0.2,−0.1, 0.1,−0.2) (bottom). Solid lines: closed-loop feedback from the regressed value function; dash-dot lines: open-loop optimum for horizon T (Pontryagin BVP).

Fig. 9: Relative validation errors for the 19-dimensional Allen–Cahn system on Ω = [−1, 1]19 as a function of N . First-order regression and second-order regression with ρ ∈ {0.5, 1.0} are compared, averaged over 5 Monte Carlo realisations. Basis IHC(4), q = 14,213, λ = 10−10. Note that each panel reports a different error metric, identified by the subscript of Err, and that the ordinate scales differ accordingly across panels.
